coredata.dc:title: Guar gum-sodium alginate nanocomposite film as a smart fluorescence-based humidity sensor: A smart packaging material
coredata.dc:creator: Rahman, Sazzadur
Chowdhury, Devasish
coredata.dc:description: Perishable packed foods are easily damaged by the change in relative humidity. In this work, we demonstrate that guar gum- sodium alginate blending with glucose-glycerol carbon dots nanocomposite film can be used to detect relative humidity. The fabricated nanocomposite film was an excellent smart sensor based on the fluorescence ‘on-off’ mechanisms against humidity. The study demonstrates that at different relative humidity conditions, such as 11 %, 33 %, 75.30 %, 84 %, and 97 %, there is a change in the fluorescence of biocomposite films under UV light. The practical feasibility of the biocomposite developed film was tested in real conditions by placing a piece of bread with high humidity conditions wrapped with the developed nanocomposite film. It was observed that under such conditions, marked quenching of fluorescence was observed and hence detection of humidity was possible. Hence, the fabricated nanocomposite film can monitor the packed food freshness using just a UV light source. Such biopolymer nanocomposite is potential materials and may find application as smart packaging materials, especially as food packaging materials.

head.ce:title: Guar gum-sodium alginate nanocomposite film as a smart fluorescence-based humidity sensor: A smart packaging material

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Material Nanochemistry Laboratory, Physical Sciences Division, Institute of Advanced Study in Science and Technology, Paschim Boragaon, Garchuk, Guwahati 781035, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Advanced Study in Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Material Nanochemistry Laboratory, Physical Sciences Division, Institute of Advanced Study in Science and Technology, Paschim Boragaon, Garchuk, Guwahati-781035, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Perishable packed foods are easily damaged by the change in relative humidity. In this work, we demonstrate that guar gum- sodium alginate blending with glucose-glycerol carbon dots nanocomposite film can be used to detect relative humidity. The fabricated nanocomposite film was an excellent smart sensor based on the fluorescence ‘on-off’ mechanisms against humidity. The study demonstrates that at different relative humidity conditions, such as 11 %, 33 %, 75.30 %, 84 %, and 97 %, there is a change in the fluorescence of biocomposite films under UV light. The practical feasibility of the biocomposite developed film was tested in real conditions by placing a piece of bread with high humidity conditions wrapped with the developed nanocomposite film. It was observed that under such conditions, marked quenching of fluorescence was observed and hence detection of humidity was possible. Hence, the fabricated nanocomposite film can monitor the packed food freshness using just a UV light source. Such biopolymer nanocomposite is potential materials and may find application as smart packaging materials, especially as food packaging materials.

ce:section.ce:para: Packaging is necessary for conserving and protecting food from deteriorating agents such as physical, chemical, microbiological, or other risks that could compromise its quality or safety [1]. Because of its ease of manufacturing and low cost, the single-use petroleum-based polymer has been employed in a variety of packaging applications in the food industry and other packaging sectors around the world [2]. But with rising concerns about environmental pollution caused by petroleum-based materials, the development of biopolymer-based products is the only alternative to eradicate single-use plastic [3]. Biopolymers are well-known environmentally friendly materials. Proteins, starch, chitosan, guar gum, sodium alginate, and pectin have all been employed in the fabrication of biodegradable films. Guar gum [4] and sodium alginate [5] are two of these polymers that have attracted attention due to their numerous benefits, including biocompatibility, superior film-forming ability, and non-toxic nature. Alginate is a naturally derived polysaccharide that consists of copolymers of β-D-mannuronic acid and α-L-guluronic with 1–4 bonds of varying composition and sequential structure [6]. It is extracted from brown algae called Phaeophyceae. Guar gum (GG) is also a polysaccharide found in the endosperm of Cyamopsis tetragonolobus or Cyamopsis psoraloides. Guar gum is composed of d-mannose units and d-galactose units. d-mannose units are linked together through β (1–4) glycoside linkage, and d-galactose units are linked with (1–6) glycoside linkage alternately.

Researchers have developed a non-toxic, biodegradable, low-cost, and environmentally friendly material for use as packaging material to replace petroleum-based material. Therefore, with the rapid advancement and development of technology, the focus has shifted towards the fabrication of smart and active packaging materials [7] to detect and report food quality in a real-time fashion. Such smart and active packaging systems respond to signals while interacting with the food-packaging environment. They may release or scavenge chemicals from the packing headspace, extending the shelf life of foods [7]. Smart and active packaging can help consumers select a fresh product without breaking the pack. Such innovative packaging boosts sales and reduces consumers' time to identify fresh food products.
Smart packaging materials with antimicrobial [8–12] antioxidants [9,10,13] and pH-sensitive biopolymer film [9,14] have been developed to increase the shelf life and monitor food freshness.

Environmental contamination and other factors, such as gas generation, humidity, and temperature variations, may occur during the processing and shipping of packaged foods before they reach customers. This may lead to damage and deterioration of the food quality inside the packet. Therefore, it is challenging to identify the fresh food items for the consumer once they are packed. In this regard, humidity plays a crucial role in preserving the quality and freshness of food. Most dry foods and perishable fruits easily deteriorate when exposed to high humidity [15]. As a result, it facilitates pathogenic microbial growth on food products and causes spoilage, and texture alteration during storage, leading to shorter shelf life and considerable economic losses [16]. Meanwhile, it is directly related to consumer health. In 2017, the World Health Organization (WHO) reported that, due to the contamination of food supplies, 600 million cases of illness were reported, and every year 420,000 people die consuming contaminated food [17]. Therefore, there is a need for the development of a humidity sensor to monitor food freshness.

Researchers have tried various approaches to fabricate materials as humidity sensors, utilizing capacitive and optical properties of the material. Graphene oxide (GO) and graphene quantum dots-based electrical and optical sensors have been well explored for humidity sensing [18–23]. However, humidity sensors of this type require a unique electrical setup as well as a dedicated power supply. As a result, there are limitations to using such sensors in real-time applications. So, there is a need to develop an instant, accessible and low-cost technique for detecting humidity for real-time analysis.

Carbon dots (CDs) are zero-dimensional nanomaterials with a size of <10 nm. CDs have excellent inherent properties, such as high aqueous solubility [24], low toxicity and higher biocompatibility. As a result, CDs have been employed in a variety of applications, including drug delivery [25], sensing [26], and bioimaging. Also, CDs have marvelous properties, as they fluoresce under UV light due to the quantum confinement effect, and the fluorescence properties can be easily tuned. Therefore, CDs are recommended for fluorescence sensing with various analytes [27]. In 2004, CDs were discovered by Xu et al. as fragments during the purification of single-walled carbon nanotubes from carbon soot using gel electrophoresis [28]. Recently, researchers have shown more interest in CDs being used in fluorescence-based sensing applications. Deb. A. et al. reported fluorescence ‘off’ based aloe vera CDs nano-bioconjugate film to detect hazardous chemicals, such as para-aminobenzoic acid (PABA), benzophenone hydroquinone, and propylparaben, used in cosmetics [29].

In this work, we have designed a prototype humidity-sensing smart biopolymer nanocomposite film from non-toxic, biodegradable, and edible-grade material. First, an attempt was made to synthesize carbon dots (CDs) by bottom-up approach with a simple microwave technique [30]. The method used is easy and environmentally friendly [30,31]. Readily available low-cost materials such as d-(+)-Glucose and glycerol were used as a precursor for the synthesis of glucose-glycerol carbon dots, labelled as CD2G. No acid, base, or organic solvent was used during the synthesis of blue fluorescent CDs. As a result, the protocol used in CD2G synthesis is green and more economically viable. After successfully synthesizing CD2G, we immobilized it in the guar gum‑sodium alginate biopolymer and used the solution casting method to fabricate a bio nanocomposite film labelled as GG-SA-CD2G nanocomposite film. The GG-SA-CD2G nanocomposite film thus could be used as a smart fluorescence ‘on-off’ sensor for sensing humidity. To demonstrate the sensing applications of the nanocomposite film, humidity sensing of nanocomposite film was studied at different percentages of relative humidity (RH), such as 11 % (very low percentage of RH), 33 %, 75.30 %, 84 %, and 97 % (very high percentage of RH). Different saturated salt solutions can be used to design different percentages of RH systems easily. The preparations are described in the next section. The advantage of such bio-nano composite film will be that such material can be used in food packaging applications. Moreover, by shining a UV light source on the bio-nanocomposite film, one can check the humidity (freshness) of the food packaging through this technique. Hence, this is the first example of incorporating functional carbon dots into biopolymer nanocomposite to fabricate a humidity sensor that has the potential to be used as a smart packaging material.

Guar gum was purchased from Himedia, India, sodium alginate and d-(+)-glucose were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich, India. Glycerol anhydrous, potassium sulfate (K2SO4), chloroform, ammonium carbonate and calcium chloride fused were purchased from Merck, India. Sodium chloride (NaCl), magnesium chloride anhydrous, (MgCl2), potassium chloride (KCl) and Lithium chloride anhydrous (LiCl) were purchased from SRL, India. Ethanol was obtained from Nucleus Scientific, India. Bread was purchased from the local market, in Assam, India.

Glucose and glycerol were used as precursors for the synthesis of CD2G. 0.72 g of glucose was dissolved in 4 mL of distilled water, followed by adding 0.36 g of glycerol. The resultant solution was mixed with a magnetic stirrer at room temperature until the solution became clear. Then the clear solution mixture was transferred into a microwave vial and subjected to microwave treatment at 180 °C for 20 min. During the microwave treatment, the pressure and power of the microwave were maintained at 14.20 bar and (20−23) W, respectively. After the microwave treatment, the resultant solution was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 30 min. The supernatant part of the solution was collected and filtered through a syringe filter, and the residual was discarded. The obtained filtered supernatant solutions were dark brown. The dark brown-colored solution was glucose-glycerol carbon dots (CD2G). The CD2G solution was stored in the fridge for further analysis and use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of guar gum- sodium alginate composite film
ce:section.ce:para: The guar gum- sodium alginate (GG-SA) composite film was fabricated using the solvent casting method. During the fabrication of the films, 0.80 g of the total weight of carbohydrates was maintained. Briefly, 0.24 g (30 wt%) of GG and 0.56 g (70 wt%) of SA were dissolved in 18 mL and 21 mL of water in separate beakers. The SA solution was added to the GG solution, and a homogeneous solution was made by stirring on a magnetic stirrer overnight at room temperature. This solution mixture was sonicated on a bath sonicator for 1 h for degassing. After degassing, the solution was cast on a 92 mm diameter Teflon plate and dried in a hot air oven for approximately 17 h. The dried film was peeled out from the Teflon plate and stored in a vacuum for further characterization. The 30 % GG (w/w) and 70 % SA (w/w) composite film is labelled as (GG-SA). Similarly, 50 % GG (w/w) and 50 % SA (w/w) labelled as (50GG-50SA), and 70 % GG (w/w) and 30 % SA (w/w) labelled as (70GG-30SA) have also been prepared. However, the GG-SA blending film showed excellent properties among the other two films. The mechanical properties of 50GG-50SA and 70GG-30SA blending films are given in the supplementary data (Fig. S1 and Table S1).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of guar gum- sodium alginate-CD2G (GG-SA-CD2G) nanocomposite film
ce:section.ce:para: The procedure for the synthesis of guar gum- sodium alginate-CD2G nanocomposite film was the same as mentioned above. In this case, after forming a completely homogeneous mixture of GG and SA solution, 2 mL of synthesized CD2G solution was added dropwise into the guar gum‑sodium alginate homogeneous mixture with continuous stirring. The CDs containing guar gum‑sodium alginate homogeneous solution were stirred overnight at RT on a magnetic stirrer at 1000 rpm. The resultant CDs containing GG-SA homogeneous solution became brown in color. This solution was sonicated on a bath sonicator for 1 h for degassing. After degassing, the solution of the final volume of ~42 mL was cast on a Teflon plate with a diameter of 92 mm and was kept for drying in a hot air oven. The temperature of the hot air oven was maintained at 55 °C for 18 h. Dried nanocomposite biopolymer film was gently peeled out from the Teflon plate and stored in a vacuum for further analysis. The fabricated nanocomposite film was labelled as GG-SA-CD2G nanocomposite film.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Humidity sensing using GG-SA-CD2G nanocomposite film
ce:section.ce:para: Different humidity chambers were constructed by using different saturated salt solutions. The salt solutions used in the study were potassium sulfate (K2SO4), sodium chloride (NaCl), magnesium chloride anhydrous (MgCl2), and Lithium chloride anhydrous (LiCl). The respective percent of relative humidity was 97 %, 75.30 %, 33 %, and 11 %. For humidity sensing, the nanocomposite biopolymer films were cut into a round shape with a diameter of 13 mm. The pieces of the nanocomposite biopolymer films were kept in the headspace of different relative humidity chambers containing the saturated salt solutions. The films were exposed for 24 h. The changes in the fluorescence intensity of the exposed nanocomposite film at different relative humidity chambers were monitored with a fluorescence spectrometer and photographed under 365 nm UV light.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Response of GG-SA-CD2G nanocomposite with other analytes
ce:section.ce:para: An experiment was carried out to study the interactions of GG-SA-CD2G nanocomposite film with other analytes, such as chloroform, ethanol and ammonia. A volume of 25 mL of chloroform, ethanol, and 5 g of ammonium carbonate as a source of ammonia was taken in a separate glass vial. The nanocomposite films with a diameter of 13 mm were placed in a headspace inside the close vial and were exposed to the analytes for 6 h at RT. After exposure to analytes, the change in fluorescence intensity of the nanocomposite films was recorded with the help of a spectrophotometer instrument.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of humidity sensing with the real food sample (bread)
ce:section.ce:para: A humidity sensing experiment of the GG-SA-CD2G nanocomposite film was performed on bread (a real sample) to show the practical applications of humidity sensing at RT. The pieces of bread were cut in dimensions of 1 × 1 cm2 and wetted with distilled water. The excess water on the bread pieces was removed with the help of blotting paper. These pieces of bread were placed in a glass vial and tightly wrapped in the GG-SA-CD2G nanocomposite film so that the inner humidity of the vial could only come into contact with the surface of the mounted film. The inner diameter and the inner depth of the glass vial were 1.50 cm and 5.30 cm. This experiment was performed at room temperature (RT) and RH of 45 %. The change in fluorescence intensity of the nanocomposite film was recorded with the help of a fluorescence spectrometer. We also took photographs under 365 nm UV light for visual detection of fluorescence intensity.

The water vapour permeability tests of the films were performed by the procedure reported by our group [4]. Briefly, 20 g of calcium chloride was taken as a desiccant, and it was heated to 200 °C for 3 h to dry. The dried anhydrous calcium chloride was filled in a 100 mL glass beaker with an inner diameter and a depth of 4.70 cm and 7 cm, respectively. The composite film was mounted on the dried anhydrous calcium chloride-containing beaker tightly, such that the water vapour passed only through the mounted composite film. After that, the beaker was kept in the sealed glass desiccator containing water. The RH inside the closed glass desiccator chamber was maintained at ~98 %. The weight of the glass beaker mounted with the composite film was recorded every 30 min for 12 h. The water vapour permeability was calculated by using Eq. (1).
(1)
WVP = qd / ts ∆ p
where, q/t = Slope of the weight increased versus time (g/s), d = Composite film thickness (cm), s = Area of the film which is exposed to moisture (69.39 cm2), ΔP = Difference of the vapour pressure between the two sides of composite films (23.76 mm Hg). The unit for WVP is g·cm·cm−2 s−1 mm Hg−1.

All the data are expressed as mean ± standard deviation, and experiments are performed at least three times. The data is analyzed by one-way analysis of variance (ANOVA) followed by all pairwise multiple comparison procedures using the Student−Newman−Keuls method. p < 0.05 was taken as the minimal level of significance.

The nanomaterials and nanocomposite films were characterized by Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrometry (Perkin Elmer FT-IR instrument) to investigate different functional groups. UV–visible absorption data for nanomaterials and composite films were recorded using the Shimadzu UV spectrophotometer (UV-2600 instruments). The optical properties of the nanomaterial and nanocomposite films were studied with Jasco, FP-8300 spectrofluorometer instrument. The particle size of the CD2G was determined by dynamic light scattering (DLS) using a Malvern ZS90 instrument. Finally, the size of the CD was confirmed by the analysis with transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image in a JEOL TEM-2100 plus model by drop-casting the CDs aqueous dispersion on a carbon-coated copper grid. X-ray diffraction (XRD) spectra were collected for the nanocomposite film on a Bruker D8 Advance diffractometer. The surface morphology of the biopolymer films was studied through atomic force microscopy (NTEGRA Prima, NT-MDT Technology) in semi-contact mode using a silicon cantilever. The thermostability of the films was studied with the thermogravimetric analyzer, and the thermograms (TGA) of the films were recorded with a Perkin-Elmer 4000 instruments. The analysis was performed with a nitrogen flow rate of 20 mL/min and in the range of 35–800 °C at a heating rate of 10 °C/min. Approximately 5–10 mg of each film was cut into small pieces and dried overnight in a vacuum oven before TGA was performed. For the mechanical study of the films, we used a universal testing machine (Tinius Olsen 5ST); the testing speed was 20 mm/min. The load cell capacity and gauge lengths were 2.5 kN and 20 mm following a standard ASTM D638 procedure. The thickness of the films was measured with the help of an electronic micrometre (Schut, Germany). The wettability of films was measured with static contact angles using a DSA 30 contact angle analyzer (Krüss GmbH, Hamburg, Germany). A color measurement instrument CM-5 Spectrophotometer (Konica Minolta, Japan), was used to measure the color of the film, and the data were expressed in CIELab scale (L*, a* and b*) in terms of lightness L* (L* = 0 for black and L* = 100 for white), a* (− = green to + = red) and b* (− = blue to + = yellow) with reflectance mode, D65 as an illuminant and a 10° observer angle. The instrument was calibrated with a white plate. The standard values of L*, a*, and b* for the white calibration plate were L* = 98.73, a* = −0.13 and b* = −0.31.

In this work, we fabricated a smart biopolymer nanocomposite film GG-SA-CD2G to detect relative humidity for monitoring food freshness by using an ‘on-off’ fluorescence mechanism. First, we synthesized glucose-glycerol carbon dots (CD2G). Carbon dots were then immobilized on guar gum‑sodium alginate biopolymers. Finally, a bio nanocomposite film was fabricated using the solution casting method. A pictorial diagram of the glucose-glycerol carbon dots preparation method is shown in Scheme 1 (A), and the fabrication of bio nanocomposite film is shown in Scheme 1 (B). A microwave synthesis reactor (Anton par) was used to synthesize carbon dots; the detailed synthesis of CD2G was discussed in the experimental section. The formation of CD2G was initially visualized by observing a bright blue fluorescence under UV light, and the aqueous dispersion solution was brown under normal light. The successful immobilization of CD2G into GG-SA biopolymer was initially visualized by its blue luminescence under UV light. The CD2G and GG-SA-CD2G biopolymer nanocomposite films were characterized using different characterization techniques. The fluorescence ‘on-off’ sensing of the fabricated smart nanocomposite film was monitored with the help of fluorescence spectroscopy in different relative humidity environments. The change in fluorescence intensity was also visualized with the naked eye by illuminating the films with UV light (365 nm). The nanocomposite film exhibited varying fluorescence intensity in different relative humidity conditions.

The functional groups on CD2G carbon dots were studied using Fourier transform-infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR). Fig. 1 (A) depicts the FT-IR spectra of CD2G carbon dots. The prominent peak around 3369 cm−1 in FT-IR spectra revealed the presence of hydroxyl (OH) groups on the CDs, suggesting their hydrophilic nature. The bands around 2936 cm−1 and 2888 cm−1 were caused by CH2 and CH stretching, respectively [32], and the bands at 1414 cm−1 correspond to CH bending vibrations [33]. The intense band at 1645 cm−1 may be due to the vibration of the CO bond. This might be due to the oxidation of OH groups during the synthesis process. The bands near 1038 cm−1 and 920 cm−1 were due to the CO stretching and OH bending vibrations, indicating the presence of residual hydroxyl groups on CDs [34].

Fig. 1 (B) shows the stacked UV–visible spectrum of CD2G. Two different absorption peaks at 227 nm and 283 nm were observed in the UV–visible spectrum. The peak at about 227 nm could result from the more energy transition of n → σ*, caused by the presence of OH groups. The prominent peak near 283 nm is due to the n-π* transition of CO present on the surfaces of the CDs. Photoluminescence studies were done on CD2G in an aqueous medium; the stacked emission spectra at different excitations are shown in Fig. 1 (C). The excitations were carried out at wavelengths ranging from 320 nm to 370 nm. As the excitation wavelength increased from 320 nm to 370 nm, the emission gradually shifted from 420 nm to 391 nm. The emission maximum was observed at 392 nm for an excitation wavelength of 360 nm. From the fluorescence spectrum of CD2G, it was observed that the spectrum is excitation-dependent fluorescence. The PL emission slightly shifted towards the blue shift. This blue shift emission is due to the different sizes and oxygen-rich groups on the surface of the CDs [35,36].

The morphology and size of the CD2G were studied using transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and dynamic light scattering (DLS). Dynamic light scattering (DLS) measurements with an average particle size of 4.93 nm validated the formation of CD2G, as shown in Fig. 1 (D). The formation of CD2G was further confirmed by TEM when the size was correlated with the DLS particle size measurement. TEM and zoom (inset) images of CD2G shown in Fig. 1 (E) reveals the nearly spherical morphology of the prepared CD2G. From the size distribution histogram shown in Fig. 1 (F), the average diameter of the CDs was 3.6 ± 0.78 nm. Therefore, the diameter of the CD2G TEM image shown in Fig. 1 (E) agrees with the DLS measurement.

Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) was done for the GG-SA film and GG-SA-CD2G nanocomposite films, as shown in Fig. 2 (A). Compared to the FT-IR spectrum of GG-SA film, the broad peak near 3200–3550 cm−1 for the GG-SA-CD2G nanocomposite film can be attributed to the stretching vibration of OH, which indicates the presence of a large number of OH groups on the surface. The absorbance band near 2885 cm−1 indicates CH stretching [37]. The Absorption band near 1710 cm−1 is assigned to CO, which may be from carbon dots. The peak shifted from 1645 cm−1 to 1710 cm−1 (from Fig. 1 (A)), i.e., the peak shifts from lower to higher wavenumber. This shift occurs due to the participation of CDs in the bonding with the monomeric units of the biopolymers in the nanocomposite film. The asymmetric and symmetric stretching of COO was attributed to absorption peaks around 1618 cm−1 and 1420 cm−1, respectively. The absorption near 1365 cm−1 and 1036 cm−1 corresponds to stretching due to CO and COC [38].

The thermal stability of GG-SA and GG-SA-CD2G nanocomposite films was also investigated. The corresponding thermogravimetric results of the films are shown in Fig. 2 (B). The first step of degradation of GG-SA film was around 35–105 °C (corresponding weight loss of 12 %), and GG-SA-CD2G, nanocomposite film was around 35–102 °C (corresponding weight loss of 3 %). Water evaporation from the polymer films causes the first step of degradation in both the films. In the second stage, the GG-SA film degraded from 232 °C to 290 °C, resulting in a 34 % weight loss. This degradation stage of the GG-SA film occurs due to the side chain degradations of the GG and SA biopolymer. The second stage of degradation for the nanocomposite film GG-SA-CD2G started from 196 °C to 333 °C, and approximately 49 % weight loss was observed. This degradation stage was attributed to the degradation of side-chain fragments of the carbon dots biopolymer nanocomposite film structure. The third stage of degradation of the films starts from 512 °C, which is due to the degradation of aromatic moieties in the film.

The X-ray diffractograms of the GG-SA film and GG-SA-CD2G nanocomposite film are shown in Fig. 2 (C). The GG-SA film exhibited two characteristic peaks at 13.32° and 21.09°. The GG-SA-CD2G nanocomposite film exhibited a distinct peak at 20.33°. After blending of GG-SA biopolymer with the CD2G, the crystallinity peak at 13.32° disappeared, and a broad peak was observed at 20.33°, suggesting a lower crystallinity of the film. Therefore, after incorporating CDs into GG-SA film, the nanocomposite film has acquired an amorphous structure, leading to lower mechanical properties of the GG-SA-CD2G nanocomposite film. Su et al. also reported a similar observation. They had reported that with the increasing crystallinity, the mechanical property increased of the film [39].

Mechanical property is crucial for materials used in packaging applications to resist the stress from external environmental factors associated with packaging. Table S2 shows the mechanical parameters of the GG-SA and GG-SA-CD2G nanocomposite films, such as mechanical strength, elongation at break (EAB), and thickness. The thickness of the GG-SA and GG-SA-CD2G nanocomposite films was 0.07 ± 0.008 mm and 0.10 ± 0.009 mm, respectively. The supporting data shows the stress and strain curves of GG-SA and GG-SA-CD2G nanocomposite film in Fig. S2. The tensile strength of the GG-SA and GG-SA-CD2G nanocomposite films was determined to be 27.43 ± 0.81 MPa and 20.50 ± 0.96 MPa, respectively. The results reveal that when CD2G is immobilized in GG-SA biopolymer, the tensile strength of the nanocomposite film decreases significantly (p < 0.05) and the EAB increases (p < 0.05). The mechanical properties of the biopolymeric carbon dots-based nanocomposite film can be changed depending on the carbon source employed in CD synthesis [40]. The decreased mechanical strength of the GG-SA-CD2G nanocomposite films compared to that of the GG-SA film is probably due to the occupancy of CD2G between the polymer chains, which facilitated the sliding of the polymer chains, and may be due to the plasticizing effect of glycerol associated with CD2G. Therefore, the flexibility between the monomeric units in the GG and SA increased, and EAB increased in the GG-SA-CD2G nanocomposite film by almost 50 % more than in the GG-SA (Table S2). Kousheh, S. A and co-workers have reported similar results that the addition of lactic acid bacteria synthesized CDs to the cellulose-based film reduced tensile strength and increased EAB extensively [8].

Tensile strength values for the nanocomposite film indicate that the mechanical properties are comparable with petroleum-based films such as HDPE (22–23 MPa) and LDPE (19–44 MPa) [41]. Moreover, according to the conventional standard, the tensile strength of packaging material must be >3.5 MPa [42]. As a result, the acquired tensile strength value of GG-SA-CD2G nanocomposite film was sufficient and may be effectively used in packaging.

An experiment was conducted to demonstrate that the nanocomposite film can withstand being kept in extremely high humid conditions (~99 % RH). The experiment was performed for 24 h at room temperature. The data are given in the electronic supplementary information (ESI) Fig. S3 (B) and Table S3. It was found that the nanocomposite film under extremely humid conditions (GG-SA-CD2G-EH) did not disintegrate. However, the samples under extremely humid conditions exhibited higher elongation break (p < 0.05) and lowered tensile strength (p < 0.05) values when compared to that obtained from the dry conditions. The tensile strength of GG-SA-CD2G-EH was determined to be 3.93 ± 0.38 MPa (electronic supplementary information (ESI), Table S3). The observed decrease in the tensile strength is because of the penetration of H2O molecules into the polymer chains. This consequently weakened the bond strength of polymers and decreased the polymer chains' compactness; however, secondary interactions like polar-polar interaction increased. Therefore, elasticity increased, leading to an increase in the elongation break value. Alvarado S. et al. have reported a similar observation [43]. Although the tensile strength of GG-SA-CD2G-EH reduced at extremely high humidity conditions, it remained more than the lower limit of tensile strength required for packaging applications [42]. This demonstrates the stability of the nanocomposite film under harsh conditions.

The morphology of GG-SA and GG-SA-CD2G composite film was investigated using atomic force microscopy (AFM) in tapping mode and Scanning Electron Microscopy (SEM, Carl Zeiss ∑igma VP' instrument) as shown in Figs. S4 and 3 , respectively. Fig. S4 shows the AFM images of the GG-SA and GG-SA-CD2G films in the planner, 3D, and surface roughness profile. After the CDs were immobilized in the biopolymer, the AFM images clearly demonstrated a reduction in surface roughness and a smoother surface. The roughness of the GG-SA film was also seen in SEM images of its surface and cross-section, as displayed in Fig. 3 (A) and (A′). This could happen due to the biopolymer molecules in the GG-SA film not being blended properly.

On the other hand, the smooth surface of GG-SA-CD2G was also visualized in the SEM image in Fig. 3 (B), where the carbon dots are clearly visible on the surface of the GG-SA-CD2G nanocomposite film. The surface morphology of GG-SA is shown in Fig. 3 A. A similar observation was noted when scanning electron microscopy was done on the cross-section of GG-SA and GG-SA-CD2G nanocomposite films as displayed in Fig. 3 A′ and B′ respectively.

The EDX-SEM spectrum of GG-SA film and GG-SA-CD2G nanocomposite film was also studied for the validation of the existence of CDs on the GG-SA-CD2G. In Fig. S7, the EDX-SEM spectra of the GG-SA and GG-SA-CD2G nanocomposite films are shown. From the spectrum, it was observed that the atomic percentage of carbon (C) and oxygen (O) of GG-SA-CD2G nanocomposite film are 62.17 % and 33.17 %, respectively, followed by sodium (Na) with 4.66. Similarly, the atomic percentage of carbon (C) and oxygen (O) of GG-SA nanocomposite films are 54.49 % and 40.53 %, respectively, followed by sodium (Na) with 4.98 %. From the EDX spectrum, it was seen that the GG-SA-CD2G nanocomposite film had a higher atomic percentage of carbon (C) than the GG-SA film. This increase in the atomic percentage of carbon in GG-SA-CD2G nanocomposite film is attributed to the incorporation of carbon nanoparticles, i.e. carbon dots, into the biopolymer composite. This further confirms the presence of carbon dots in the GG-SA-CD2G nanocomposite film. However, a small amount of sodium was observed which originates from sodium alginate present in the polymer composite film.

A UV–visible absorption spectrum was obtained for the GG-SA-CD2G nanocomposite film. The absorbance spectrum of the nanocomposite film is shown in Fig. 4 (A), which exhibits two distinct absorption maxima. The peak at 220 nm corresponds to the n → σ* transition, which requires more energy and demonstrates the existence of the OH group in the nanocomposite film. The absorption peak at 293 nm is due to the n → π* transition due to the presence of the CO group. After immobilizing carbon dots into the GG-SA-CD2G composite film, the film exhibited blue fluorescence under the illumination of UV light.

The fluorescent GG-SA-CD2G nanocomposite film was studied using a photoluminescence spectrofluorometer which was conducted using a solid setup. The films were excited from 350 nm to 390 nm, and the emission spectra were recorded, as shown in Fig. 4 (B). The maximum emission peak was obtained when excited at 370 nm. The image of GG-SA-CD2G nanocomposite film under UV light (365 nm) is displayed in the inset of Fig. 4 (B).

For both the GG-SA film and the GG-SA-CD2G nanocomposite films, UV–visible spectra were also performed in transmittance mode and are shown in Fig. 4. (C). It was observed that, after the successful incorporation of CD2G carbon dots into the GG-SA film, the % of transmittance of UV light of the nanocomposite film decreased as compared to GG-SA film. Similar results have been reported by our group earlier [44]. Konwar et al. demonstrated that after incorporating carbon dots into the chitosan polymer film, the nanocomposite film's transmittance in the UV region is reduced. Interestingly, it was found that the GG-SA-CD2G nanocomposite film can completely reduce the transmittance in UV in the range of 200 nm to 280 nm. This may be due to the absorption of UV light by the high number of OH and CO groups present on CD2G carbon dots in the nanocomposite film.
Therefore, the GG-SA-CD2G nanocomposite film can exhibit a suitable UV shielding capability, preventing the deterioration of food due to UV radiation.

The film's color plays an important role in the food packaging applications indicating the product's appearance, which directly influences the selection of food items by the consumers. Color measurement values of the GG-SA and GG-SA-CD2G composite films are shown in Table 1 . Both the films were transparent; however, the L* value demonstrated that GG-SA film was lighter than the GG-SA-CD2G. After immobilizing carbon dots into the biopolymer, the a* value of GG-SA-CD2G nanocomposite film decreased (p = 0.61), indicating that the film was slightly greenish than the GG-SA film. An increase in the b* value of GG-SA-CD2G nanocomposite film as compared to the GG-SA film indicates the increased yellow color of the nanocomposite film.

The photographs of the GG-SA film and GG-SA-CD2G nanocomposite film are shown in Fig. 4 (D) and (E). Both the films were transparent, but the GG-SA-CD2G nanocomposite film was comparatively less transparent than the GG-SA film.

Contact angle measurement was done for the GG-SA film and GG-SA-CD2G nanocomposite films, shown in Figs. 4 (F) and 7 (G). The water contact angle measurements were taken on the films to study the film's wettability. The GG-SA film and GG-SA-CD2G nanocomposite films have contact angles of 76.1° and 63°, respectively. The contact angle value of the GG-SA film reduces after blending with CD2G. As a result, it can be inferred from the contact angle measurement data that the GG-SA-CD2G nanocomposite film is more hydrophilic than the GG-SA film. This interesting result further revealed that plenty of hydrophilic functional groups were present on the GG-SA-CD2G nanocomposite film surface, which can readily interact with the water molecules present in different humidity environments.

A water vapour permeability (WVP) experiment was conducted to determine the water vapour permeability through the composite film. The experiments were carried out on the GG-SA and GG-SA-CD2G composite film, and the results were summarized in table S4. It was found that the calculated WVP value of the GG-SA-CD2G nanocomposite film was more than the GG-SA composite film. These results further revealed the presence of many hydrophilic groups on the surface of the nanocomposite film. Since WVP is mainly dependent on the hydrophilicity of the material [45], water vapour is more diffusible through the nanocomposite film than the GG-SA composite film. However, the obtained WVP values are significantly smaller than already reported in the literature for potentially used biopolymers in food packaging [46,47].

The fluorescent GG-SA-CD2G nanocomposite films demonstrate an optical sensor detecting different relative humidity (RH). The experiment was performed at room temperature and relative humidity at ~45 %. Here, 45 % RH was taken as a standard relative humidity condition to distinguish the lower & higher % of RH during the experiment. The fabricated GG-SA-CD2G nanocomposite film is an excellent humidity sensor for both high and low humidity conditions. The experiment was carried out for the different controlled relative humidity, such as 97 %, 84 %, 75.30 %, 33 %, and 11 %, respectively. The controlled RH environments were achieved by using different saturated salt solutions. Different salts like potassium sulfate (K2SO4), potassium chloride (KCl), sodium chloride (NaCl), magnesium chloride anhydrous (MgCl2), lithium chloride anhydrous (LiCl), and their saturated solutions yielded the RH of 97 %, 84 %, 75.30 %, 33 %, and 11 %, respectively in a closed glass container at room temperature. The sensing at different RH by the nanocomposite film was monitored using spectrophotometer instruments, which is shown in Fig. 5 . Fig. 5 (A), (B), (C), (D), and (E) show the stacked PL emission spectra of GG-SA-CD2G nanocomposite sensing with different relative humidity, such as 97 %, 84 %, 75.30 %, 33 %, and 11 % respectively. The PL spectrum revealed that at high RH, the fluorescence intensity decreases, and complete quenching occurs at 97 % RH. However, at low humidity, such as 11 % RH, the fluorescence intensity of the film increases. At RH 97 %, the fluorescence intensity quenches 100 %. At 84 % RH and 75.30 % RH, the fluorescence intensity quenches 88.85 % and 63.57 %, respectively. At low humidity, such as at 11 % and 33 % RH, the percent of fluorescence enhancement is obtained and was calculated as 27 % and 16.66 %, respectively. The mechanism of change in fluorescence intensity of GG-SA-CD2G nanocomposite at different % of RH will be discussed in the ‘mechanistic insight section’. This fascinating ‘on-off’ phenomenon could be used for monitoring the different relative humidity of the surroundings of the nanocomposite film as a food packaging material. The corresponding integration of the PL spectra for PL emission spectra of GG-SA-CD2G nanocomposite films at initial and after treatment with different relative humidity is given in inset Fig. 5.

Moreover, the fluorescence intensity of the nanocomposite film at different RH can be visualized by the naked eye with the help of illumination under UV light. Fig. 5 A (ii), B (ii), C (ii), D (ii), and E (ii) displays the photographs of the illuminated nanocomposite films. Since the nanocomposite film gives the maximum emission intensity when excited at 370 nm. Hence, during the RH sensing experiment, we experimented by exciting at the same wavelength. Interestingly, we found that with increasing RH around the nanocomposite film, the fluorescence intensity of the film decreased and vice versa.

The fluorescence intensity change from maximum PL emission intensity of the nanocomposite film at different RH was displayed in a histogram plot in Fig. 6 . The quenching of the fluorescence intensity of the nanocomposite film takes place while the RH increases, and the fluorescence intensity of the nanocomposite film increases while the RH decreases. This significant detectable fluorescence intensity at both low and high RH by the nanocomposite film was demonstrated in the histogram plot. Thus, the GG-SA-CD2G nanocomposite film can monitor RH, indirectly food freshness, as it is influenced by the change in RH.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Humidity sensing experiment with a real sample, a food item (bread)
ce:section.ce:para: In order to demonstrate the practical applicability of RH sensing by the GG-SA-CD2G nanocomposite film, an experiment was performed by taking a food sample (bread). Fresh bread was bought from the local market for the study. The fresh bread was cut into 1 × 1 cm2 dimension pieces. The pieces of bread were wetted with distilled water, and the excess water was removed with the help of blotting paper. The wetted pieces of bread were kept inside the glass vial and mounted with GG-SA-CD2G nanocomposite film, as shown in Fig. 7 A (i & ii) and B (i & ii). The experiment was carried out for 1 and 6 h at RT and maintained RH ~ 45 %. After keeping the wetted bread inside the glass vial, the RH inside the vial continuously increased. Therefore, water molecules were bound with the nanocomposite film through hydrogen bonding. Consequently, the quenching of the fluorescence of the nanocomposite film takes place. Fluorescence emission spectra were recorded at 370 nm excitation for initial dry nanocomposite film and mounted CDs nanocomposite film after 1 and 6 h, as shown in Fig. 7 (C). The maximum emission spectra were recorded at wavelength 445 nm. The emission fluorescence spectra of nanocomposite film after keeping for 1 h and 6 h showed a quenched emission peak. Interestingly, the fluorescence quenching can also be visualized by the naked eyes under UV light (365 nm) illumination. The photographs of the initial fluoresced nanocomposite film and, after 1 h and 6 h under UV light are shown in Fig. 7 A (i) ➔ A (ii) and B (i) ➔ B (ii).

The mechanism of fluorescence quenching of CDs nanocomposite film in the presence of humidity can be understood by the proposed mechanism, as shown in Fig. S5. After the immobilization of CDs, the biopolymer chains entrap the CDs within their polymer network structure, and the nanocomposite films show blue fluorescence under UV light. At high relative humidity, the number of water molecules around the film increases, leading to increased interactions of water molecules with the biopolymeric nanocomposite film through hydrogen bonding. Consequently, in the presence of water, the COONa group of sodium alginate gets dissociated into its ionic form, Na+ and –COO− [48]. Under such a scenario, interaction takes place between the –OH groups containing CD2G carbon dots with the ionized –COO− groups in the SA polymer chains. The interaction occurs through electrostatic and dipole-dipole interactions apart from their normal hydrogen bonding interactions, as shown in Fig. S5. Consequently, the CDs lose their fluorescence property, leading to fluorescence quenching in the nanocomposite films. In contrast, at a very low percentage of RH, i.e., RH near 11 %, the film becomes dry, and there is almost no presence of water molecules; thus, the fluorescence intensity of the nanocomposite film slightly increases when compared to 45 % RH conditions (under normal experimental condition). Our group has demonstrated similar observations earlier [49].

The fluorescence intensity of CDs could be tuned by bonding with bifunctional chain molecules. In addition, the optical properties of the CDs are also influenced by the hydrogen bonding and the internal penetration of the protic solvents. Zhang et al. reported that the effect of hydrogen bonding in fluorophores containing nitrogen with the protic solvent is more than in aprotic solvents [50]. These interactions encourage internal conversion, increase spectral shifts, shorten PL lifetimes, lower quantum yield values, and decrease fluorescence intensity [51]. Therefore, more water molecules participate in hydrogen bonding with the CDs at high humidity leading to fluorescence quenching.

As discussed in the above section, one of the major reasons for decreasing PL intensity is hydrogen bonding and the effect of protic solvents. So, a separate study was conducted to investigate the PL response in presence of solvents like chloroform, ethanol, and ammonia, which can interact similarly with GG-SA-CD2G nanocomposite film. It was observed that when the GG-SA-CD2G nanocomposite film is exposed for 6h to chloroform, ethanol, and ammonia vapour, a slight quenching of PL intensity is observed. The percentage of quenching of PL intensity of exposed nanocomposite film with ethanol, chloroform and ammonia vapour from the neat film was 8.36 %, 8.45 %, and 16.86 %, respectively. So, it reiterates the mechanism proposed. Fig. S6 (A), (B), and (C) displays the fluorescence emission spectra of the GG-SA-CD2G nanocomposite film in the presence of chloroform, ethanol, and ammonia vapour. It is believed that in real cases, the concentration of chloroform, alcohol, and ammonia would be very less. Hence, it will not pose any interference in the system.

The detection of relative humidity (RH) is very much essential to monitor the food's freshness. Here we fabricate a fluorescent guar gum‑sodium alginate glucose-glycerol carbon dots nanocomposite film through a solution casting method. The nanocomposite film shows good mechanical strength of 20.50 ± 0.96 MPa, and the elongation at the break of the nanocomposite film increased by almost 50 % than the GG-SA film. Further, the nanocomposite film showed good thermal stability. The nanocomposite film can monitor the relative humidity at low and high humidity via the ‘on-off’ fluorescence. At high RH humidity, fluorescence quenching occurs, and at low humidity, the fluorescence intensity of the nanocomposite film increases. The change in fluorescence intensity of the nanocomposite film at different RH was monitored using a fluorescence spectrophotometer. Moreover, the naked eye visualization of the change in fluorescence intensity is possible for high RH (>45 %) and low humidity (<45 %) under illumination in 365 nm UV light. The practical utility of the synthesized nanocomposite film was demonstrated with fresh food such as “bread”. The high RH humidity of the “bread” can be determined using the fluorescence property of nanocomposite film (observed decrease in fluorescence intensity). Therefore, the fabricated nanocomposite film could be a promising smart food packaging material to monitor the freshness of the food without opening the packaging.
